comments:
This paper presents S-Net, an extraction-then-generation pipeline for reading comprehension on MS-MARCO: (i) an R-Net / pointer-network based extractor that finds evidence spans and (ii) a sequence-to-sequence generator that synthesizes the final answer, with the extracted span encoded as an extra feature. The idea is well-motivated because MS-MARCO answers are not constrained to be exact spans; combining extraction and generation is a sensible approach and the paper shows improvements in ROUGE-L and BLEU-1 over extraction-only baselines and several reported systems.

Strengths: The pipeline is simple and practical, leverages strong existing components (gated attention, pointer nets, attention-based decoder), and demonstrates empirical gains on a large real-world dataset. The case study examples illustrate concrete benefits (refinement, inference, boundary control).

Weaknesses / concerns: (1) The training data selection relies on heuristics (keeping only examples whose best-span ROUGE-L exceeds thresholds) which may bias results; the implications of this filtering are not fully analyzed. (2) Handling of multi-evidence and multi-passage questions is left unaddressed, though MS-MARCO often requires aggregating across passages. (3) Evaluation is limited to BLEU-1 and ROUGE-L (official metrics) and automatic metrics only; there is no human evaluation or qualitative error analysis beyond a few examples. (4) The comparison lacks deeper ablation studies (e.g., effect of the evidence-feature embedding, impact of using predicted vs. gold spans during generator training, statistical significance). (5) The decoder seems to rely on ad-hoc post-processing for <unk> and duplication, which should be more systematically handled (copy mechanisms / pointer-generator alternatives). (6) The manuscript contains duplicated and repetitive text fragments and minor presentation issues that reduce clarity.

Suggestions for improvement: include ablations showing importance of the evidence feature and training-data filtering; evaluate robustness when multiple passages/evidences are present; compare against a strong copy-enabled generator (or integrate pointer-generator) and report per-category scores and statistical significance; add human evaluation or more extensive qualitative analysis; clean up presentation and remove duplicated paragraphs.

Overall, the work is a useful, pragmatic contribution addressing generation-style answers in reading comprehension, but would benefit from stronger analysis and clearer presentation.
